<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Identifying CSS specificity regressions**
</details></think>

_**Agent (gpt-5.3-codex 2026-02-16 13:32:41Z)**_

**Findings**
1. Medium: Several refactors from comma-separated selectors to `:is(...)` changed selector specificity for part of the group, so this is not behavior-preserving and can break downstream overrides.  
`packages/rich-renderer-video/src/styles.css:102`, `apps/web/src/styles/layer.css:21`, `apps/web/src/styles/variables.css:11`, `apps/web/src/styles/theme.css:37`, `packages/rich-renderer-codeblock/src/styles.css:134`, `packages/rich-renderer-embed/src/styles-static.css:141`  
Example: old ` .dark .rr-video-controls` was less specific than `:root.dark .rr-video-controls`; new `:is(.dark, :root.dark) .rr-video-controls` always takes the higher specificity branch, making `.dark .rr-video-controls` overrides harder/impossible unless specificity is increased.

**Open question**
1. Was the specificity increase intentional? If not, keep mixed-specificity selectors split, and only collapse groups into `:is(...)` when all alternatives have equal specificity.

**Summary**
Only one substantive risk found: cascade/specificity regressions from mixed-specificity `:is(...)` rewrites. No runtime tests were run; this should be validated with a quick visual regression pass on theming/custom-style override scenarios.

---
